metadata.title: Instrument Loop Hierarchy & Folder Naming Rules
metadata.description: How control loops are organized, why the folder name IS an instrument tag, what the P&ID symbol shapes mean, and how to reason about field vs DCS instruments. Used by all AI agents to understand loop folder structure.
metadata.source: ISA 5.1-2024, field engineering practice, Horizon Sparks domain knowledge

loop_hierarchy.description: A control loop is a TEAM of instruments working together. They share a loop number. The controller is the 'boss' — it names the loop and the folder. But the controller may or may not be a physical field device.
loop_hierarchy.principle: The FOLDER NAME is an instrument tag. Parse it the same way you parse any tag: prefix-TYPE-loop-suffix. The TYPE tells you what kind of loop this is and what field instruments you should expect inside.

folder_name_rules.rule_1: The folder name IS the primary controller tag (e.g., 201C-FIC-2131-01 = Flow Indicating Controller, loop 2131)
folder_name_rules.rule_2: Parse the folder name to extract the instrument type. This tells you the loop's measured variable and function.
folder_name_rules.rule_3: The controller CAN be inside the folder (if it's field-mounted) or NOT inside the folder (if it's a DCS/PLC function). BOTH are normal.
folder_name_rules.rule_4: If the controller tag appears in the folder's tag list → field-mounted controller. Normal.
folder_name_rules.rule_5: If the controller tag does NOT appear in the folder's tag list → DCS/PLC function. Also normal. NOT a gap.
folder_name_rules.rule_6: The folder contains the FIELD instruments of the loop — whatever is physically at the pipe, vessel, or equipment.

how_to_reason.step_1: Read the folder name. Parse the instrument type (e.g., FIC, TIC, LIC, PIC).
how_to_reason.step_2: Determine the measured variable from the first letter (F=Flow, T=Temperature, P=Pressure, L=Level, etc.).
how_to_reason.step_3: Look at what's INSIDE the folder. These are the field instruments.
how_to_reason.step_4: Check if the controller tag is inside. If yes = field controller. If no = DCS controller. Either is fine.
how_to_reason.step_5: Check if the expected field instruments for this loop type are present (transmitter, element, valve, etc.).
how_to_reason.step_6: Evaluate documentation completeness based on the FIELD instruments, not the controller.

examples_by_variable.description: The pattern is UNIVERSAL for process-variable loops (F, T, P, L, A, S, W, V). For non-measurement tags (X=on/off, H=hand, Z=position, Y=relay), the first letter indicates function type, not measured variable. These are examples, not the only possibilities.

flow_loop.controller: FIC (may or may not be in field)
expected_field_instruments: FE (Flow Element — orifice plate, mechanical, no cable)
FIT or FT (Flow Transmitter — 4-20mA, has cable)
FV (Flow Control Valve — positioner, has cable)
flow_loop.note: FE is mechanical (no cable needed). FIT and FV need cables.

temperature_loop.controller: TIC (may or may not be in field)
temperature_loop.note: TW is mechanical (no cable). TE often shares cable with TT.

pressure_loop.controller: PIC (may or may not be in field)
expected_field_instruments: PI (Pressure Indicator — local if no cable, remote if cable)
pressure_loop.note: PI and PG may both exist on same loop. PG is always mechanical.

level_loop.controller: LIC (may or may not be in field)
expected_field_instruments: LG (Level Gauge — sight glass, mechanical, no cable)
level_loop.note: LG is always mechanical. LG with cable data = unusual (remote indicator on tall tower).

on_off_valve_loop.controller: XV is the valve itself (field device)
expected_field_instruments: XV (On/Off Valve — the actuated valve body)
on_off_valve_loop.note: XV loops are different — the 'controller' IS the field device. XV is always in the folder.

relief_device.meaning: Pressure Safety Valve — relief device, loop 2131
relief_device.controller: PSV is NOT a controller — it is a mechanical protective device. The folder is named after the device, not a control function.
expected_field_instruments: PSV (Spring-loaded relief valve — mechanical, no cable)
relief_device.note: PSV folders typically contain only the PSV itself. This is normal — relief valves are standalone mechanical devices. If pressure switches (PSH/PSL) exist for monitoring, they may be in a separate loop.

safety_shutdown_loop.meaning: Pressure Switch High — safety shutdown, loop 2131
safety_shutdown_loop.controller: Safety logic is in the SIS/ESD system (not a field controller)
expected_field_instruments: PSH or PSL (Pressure Switch — discrete input, has cable)
XV or SDV (Shutdown Valve — discrete output, has cable)
safety_shutdown_loop.note: Safety loops may have redundant switches (2oo3 voting). Multiple PSH tags in one loop is valid. The safety logic is in the SIS, not a field controller.

analyzer_loop.controller: AIC (may or may not be in field)
expected_field_instruments: AE (Analyzer Element/Probe — may share cable with AT)
analyzer_loop.note: Analyzer loops often have complex sample systems.

motor_loop.controller: HS is a field device (start/stop button)
motor_loop.note: Motor loops mix instrument and power elements.

pid_symbol_shapes.description: The shape of the instrument symbol on a P&ID tells you WHERE the instrument lives. This affects whether it appears in tag extraction and whether it should be in the loop folder.

circle_no_line.location: Field — physically at the pipe, vessel, or equipment
circle_no_line.description: Field-mounted instrument. This is what YOLO extracts from P&IDs.

circle_solid_line.location: Primary location — operator-accessible (control room, local panel, or main board)

circle_in_square.description: Software function in DCS. NOT a physical instrument. This is why controllers often don't appear in extraction data.

diamond_in_square.description: PLC software instruction. NOT a physical instrument.

circle_dashed_line.description: Physical instrument behind a panel. Requires opening enclosure to access.

pid_symbol_shapes.extraction_implication: YOLO+OCR primarily detects circles (field instruments). Circle-in-square (DCS functions) and diamond-in-square (PLC functions) are different visual patterns and may not be in extraction results. This is NORMAL — the AI should not flag missing DCS tags as errors.

never_flag_as_missing: Controller tag (FIC, TIC, PIC, LIC, AIC, SIC) not found in tag list — it's likely a DCS function
Alarm tag (PAH, TAH, LAH, PAHH) not in tag list — it's a DCS software alarm
Recorder tag (FR, TR, PR, LR) not in tag list — it's often a DCS historian function
always_check: Parse the FOLDER NAME as a tag to understand the loop type
Check if field instruments (transmitters, elements, valves) are present
For each field instrument, check if it has the documentation it needs (Cable Schedule, I/O List)
Remember: the controller NAMES the loop but may not be a FIELD device
common_mistakes_to_avoid: Saying 'no controllers found' when the folder name IS the controller
Saying 'missing FIC' when FIC is a DCS function shown as circle-in-square
Treating every instrument type the same — some are field, some are DCS, some are mechanical
Assuming the folder name is NOT inside the folder — field-mounted controllers ARE in the tag list
Using only flow loop examples — the pattern applies to ALL measured variables (T, P, L, A, S, etc.)
